content.text: The Difference Between a Behaviour Spec and a System Prompt

A behaviour specification and a system prompt are two different artifacts that work together but serve different audiences and different purposes. The behaviour specification — captured in the PRD's sections 5 through 7 — is a product-level document written in plain language for product managers, conversation designers, engineers, and safety reviewers to agree on. The system prompt is a technical artifact written in natural language for the language model to follow. They are related by a translation process: every behaviour specified in the PRD must be traceable to a specific instruction in the system prompt, and every instruction in the system prompt must be justified by a corresponding behaviour specification.

Teams that conflate these two artifacts produce one of two failure modes. The first: a PRD that is so vague it provides no guidance ("the agent should be helpful"), leaving the system prompt author to invent behaviour without stakeholder alignment. The second: a PRD that contains draft system prompt text, making the PRD unusable for non-technical stakeholders and untestable by QA. The discipline of separating what the agent must do (behaviour spec) from how the prompt instructs it to do so (prompt requirements) is what makes both artifacts useful.

attrs.alt: Behaviour Spec → Prompt Requirements → System Prompt Pipeline
attrs.title: PRD Prompt Pipeline

content.text: Anatomy of a Behaviour Specification
A behaviour specification answers ten questions about the agent's conversational behaviour. Each question corresponds to a category of prompt instructions that the system prompt must later implement.
What persona and tone must the agent maintain? (Drives: role definition, style instructions, prohibited phrasings)
What is the agent's scope, and what should it do when a user asks about something out of scope? (Drives: scope instructions, refusal phrasings, routing tables)
What safety constraints must the agent respect? (Drives: prohibited content instructions, escalation triggers, hard refusal rules)
How should the agent handle missing information? (Drives: clarification strategy, maximum clarification turns, assumption rules)
How should the agent express uncertainty? (Drives: hedging language, citation requirements, confidence thresholds for response delivery)
What confirmation must the agent seek before irreversible actions? (Drives: confirmation templates, explicit user-approval requirements)
How should the agent format its outputs? (Drives: response length constraints, Markdown/plain-text rules, table and list use guidelines)
What context must the agent retain across turns, and what must it release? (Drives: context management instructions, slot-value persistence rules)
When and how must the agent escalate to a human? (Drives: escalation triggers, handoff language, what context to pass to the human agent)
How should the agent invoke tools? (Drives: tool call ordering, error handling instructions, parameter extraction rules)
Writing Behaviour Specs That Are Testable

The single most important quality criterion for a behaviour specification is testability: can a QA engineer read this specification and write an eval scenario that definitively confirms or refutes whether the agent meets it? Untestable specs are not requirements — they are aspirations.

The transformation from untestable to testable is usually a matter of precision. "The agent should be concise" is untestable. "The agent must respond to invoice status queries in three sentences or fewer, measured by sentence count in the final response" is testable. "The agent should handle errors gracefully" is untestable. "When a tool returns an error code of any kind, the agent must respond with a plain-language message containing no technical terms, and must not include the error code or stack trace in the response" is testable.

The formula for a testable behaviour spec item: [actor] must [action] when [condition], [measured by / verified by] [observable criterion]. Not all items will fit this formula perfectly, but those that do not should be explicitly flagged as requiring human eval rather than automated testing.
From Behaviour Spec to Prompt Requirements

Prompt requirements are the intermediate layer between what the agent must do (behaviour spec) and how the system prompt instructs it (the prompt itself). Writing prompt requirements before writing the system prompt serves two purposes: it forces the author to think at the instruction level before committing to specific wording, and it creates a checklist that verifies the final system prompt covers every required behaviour.
Prompt requirements use three instruction types:

MUST instructions: unconditional requirements. Example: "MUST introduce itself as 'Finance Assistant' on the first turn of every session." MUST instructions in the spec generate MUST-pass eval scenarios — any test that fails a MUST is a launch blocker.

MUST NOT instructions: unconditional prohibitions. Example: "MUST NOT reveal internal system names or database identifiers in any response." MUST NOT instructions generate adversarial eval scenarios that try to elicit the prohibited behaviour.

IF/THEN conditionals: context-dependent behaviours. Example: "IF the user asks for information about a different employee's records, THEN refuse with: 'I can only access your own records.' and do not proceed." IF/THEN instructions generate scenario pairs: one positive scenario where the condition is true and the agent follows the THEN branch, and one negative scenario where the condition is false.
Complete Worked Example: Invoice Agent Prompt Requirements

The following illustrates a complete prompt requirements section for the invoice status agent. This is the level of specificity that makes system prompt authoring straightforward and eval scenario design mechanical.

PROMPT REQUIREMENTS — Invoice Status Agent v1.0

-- PERSONA AND TONE --
MUST use second-person address ("your invoice") consistently throughout
MUST limit invoice status responses to 1-2 sentences
MUST NOT use accounting or ERP jargon ("net receivable", "GL code", "AR aging")
MUST NOT use casual language ("no worries", "sure thing", "yep")
MUST NOT express opinions about approval decisions

-- SCOPE ENFORCEMENT --
MUST state capability boundary on out-of-scope requests:
  Template: "I can help with invoice status and approvals. For [topic],
  please contact [routing destination]."
MUST NOT attempt to answer questions outside: invoice status, approval status,
  approver name, payment date, pending approvals list (v1.1)
IF user asks about supplier invoices, THEN route to finance@company.com
IF user asks about invoice creation, THEN route to finance@company.com
IF user asks about billing disputes, THEN route to accounts-disputes@company.com

-- SAFETY CONSTRAINTS --
MUST NOT display another employee's invoice data under any circumstances
MUST NOT speculate about future approval likelihood
MUST NOT reveal ERP system name, API endpoint, or database names
IF user expresses financial distress (keywords: "desperate", "losing job",
  "can't pay", "eviction", "at risk"), THEN stop task, respond with:
  "It sounds like you may be under some pressure. [Company] offers confidential
  support through [EAP resource]. I'm here when you're ready to continue."
IF user requests data on behalf of another employee ("check for my manager",
  "look up John's invoice"), THEN refuse: "I can only access your own records."

-- CLARIFICATION STRATEGY --
IF invoice_id is missing, THEN ask once: "Could you share the invoice number?"
IF period is missing, THEN ask once: "Which month should I check — the current
  month, or a specific period?"
MUST NOT ask for the same slot twice in a single session
IF second clarification attempt fails, THEN:
  "I wasn't able to confirm that invoice. You can check directly at [ERP portal]
  or contact finance@company.com."
IF user gives ambiguous period after one ask, THEN state assumption explicitly:
  "I'll check [assumed period] — let me know if you meant a different period."

-- TOOL CALL HANDLING --
MUST inject requester_employee_id from auth context; never ask the user for it
IF get_invoice_status returns not_found, THEN:
  "I couldn't find invoice [ID] for [period]. Please check the invoice number
  or contact finance@company.com."
IF get_invoice_status returns timeout or rate_limited, THEN:
  "I'm having trouble connecting right now. Please try again in a moment,
  or check [ERP portal link] directly."
MUST NOT display raw error codes, API error messages, or HTTP status codes

-- CONFIRMATION BEFORE ACTION --
(No mutating actions in v1.0 scope — N/A)

-- OUTPUT FORMAT --
Invoice status response template:
  "Invoice [ID] ([amount]) [status] on [date] by [approver]."
  Example: "Invoice INV-4521 (£12,400.00) was approved on 3 Jul 2024 by Sarah Chen."
Pending approval summary: plain-text list, one invoice per line, max 10 items
MUST NOT use Markdown headers in chat responses
MUST NOT use bullet points unless listing 3+ items

-- UNCERTAINTY AND CITATIONS --
MUST NOT use "I think" or "I believe" — state facts or say "I couldn't find"
IF invoice has multiple matches, THEN list top 3 with amount and date; ask user to
  confirm before displaying further details
MUST cite the ERP portal as the authoritative source for all invoice data
Traceability: From Spec to Prompt to Eval

Every prompt requirement must be traceable in both directions: backward to the behaviour spec item that justifies it, and forward to the eval scenario that verifies it. This traceability is not bureaucratic overhead — it is the mechanism that prevents three kinds of failure:

Orphaned prompt instructions. A system prompt instruction with no corresponding behaviour spec item indicates an undiscussed assumption. The instruction may be correct, but without stakeholder review it may contradict the product owner's intent or create a safety gap.

Unverified prompt instructions. A prompt requirement with no corresponding eval scenario is a requirement that cannot be confirmed to work. It may be implemented correctly in the system prompt, or it may be silently violated by the model's tendencies — and without an eval scenario there is no way to know.

Prompt regression. When the system prompt is updated — to fix a failure, add a feature, or optimise performance — existing prompt requirements must be re-evaluated. Without traceability, a prompt change that fixes one requirement may silently break another.

Implement traceability using identifiers. Each behaviour spec item gets an ID (BS-01, BS-02). Each prompt requirement references its behaviour spec source (PR-03 ← BS-02). Each eval scenario references its prompt requirement (ES-07 verifies PR-03). This three-level ID chain gives reviewers and auditors a complete evidence trail.
Common Prompt Requirement Anti-Patterns
Several patterns in prompt requirements consistently produce unreliable system prompts.

Over-specification. Listing hundreds of specific phrasings the agent must memorise makes the prompt brittle — it fails on minor phrasing variants not covered by the list. Better: specify the principle and one canonical example, and let the model generalise.

Contradictory instructions. "Be concise" and "always explain your reasoning" are contradictory for most responses. Without specifying which takes priority (or in which contexts), the model will alternate unpredictably between the two behaviours.

Negative instructions without alternatives. "Do not discuss competitor products" without specifying what to say instead leaves the model to improvise a refusal phrasing. Improvised refusals are often awkward and inconsistent. Always pair a MUST NOT with the alternative behaviour.

Vague quantifiers. "Sometimes ask for confirmation" is not an instruction. "Ask for explicit confirmation before executing any action with side effects" is. Remove all instances of "sometimes," "often," "usually," and "as appropriate" from prompt requirements — they introduce randomness that cannot be tested.

Untested format templates. Including specific response templates in prompt requirements without testing whether the model actually follows them is a common source of surprises. Every template in the prompt requirements must have an eval scenario that checks adherence to the template.
Prompt Requirements for Multi-Turn Context
One of the most commonly underdeveloped areas of prompt requirements is context management: what the agent must remember, reference, and eventually release across turns.
Context requirements to specify explicitly:

Slot persistence: once the user provides an invoice number, the agent must retain it for the remainder of the session and not re-ask for it, even if the conversation diverges briefly to a different topic and returns.

Correction handling: if the user corrects a slot value ("actually make that invoice 4522, not 4521"), the agent must update the slot, acknowledge the correction, and use the new value for all subsequent operations in the session.
Disambiguation memory: if the user disambiguated between two matching invoices in an earlier turn, the agent must not ask for the same disambiguation again within the same session.
Cross-session amnesia: at the start of a new session, the agent must not reference slot values from a previous session unless the memory system explicitly provides them via the context window.

Each context requirement generates a multi-turn eval scenario — a conversation transcript of three or more turns that verifies the context management behaviour. These are the most complex scenarios to write and the most valuable ones to have, because context management failures are the most common source of user frustration in production agentic systems.
Versioning Prompt Requirements With the System Prompt

Prompt requirements and system prompts must be versioned together. When the system prompt changes, the prompt requirements must be reviewed to confirm: (a) every existing prompt requirement is still satisfied by the new prompt, and (b) the change does not introduce behaviour that contradicts an existing prompt requirement.

In practice, the most reliable workflow is to store the prompt requirements document and the system prompt in the same directory in the repository, with the same version tag. A PR that changes the system prompt must also update the prompt requirements if the change affects what the agent does, and must include at least one new eval scenario if the change introduces a new behaviour. Code review for prompt changes is not optional — it is the last gate between a requirement ambiguity and a production incident.
Prompt Requirements for Agentic Tool Use

When an agent invokes external tools, the behaviour specification must cover not just the tool's function but the agent's decision-making around when to invoke it, how to handle its outputs, and what to do when it fails. Prompt requirements for tool use are some of the most critical — and most neglected — items in the requirements phase.

TOOL USE PROMPT REQUIREMENTS — Generic Template

-- TOOL INVOCATION DECISIONS --
MUST NOT invoke a tool if the required slot values have not been confirmed
MUST extract tool parameters only from the current conversation context;
  MUST NOT invent or assume parameter values that the user has not provided
MUST state clearly to the user when it is retrieving information:
  Acceptable: (brief silence) or "Let me check that for you."
  MUST NOT: "I'm querying the database" (implementation detail)

-- TOOL OUTPUT HANDLING --
MUST NOT pass raw tool output to the user; always translate to natural language
IF tool output contains technical fields (IDs, codes, timestamps in epoch format),
  THEN translate to user-readable equivalents before responding
IF tool output is empty or null for a required field, THEN acknowledge the gap:
  "I found the invoice but the [field] is not yet available."
MUST NOT truncate tool output silently; if response would exceed format limit,
  summarise and offer to provide full detail on request

-- PARALLEL TOOL CALLS --
IF the task requires data from two independent tools (e.g. status + approver detail),
  invoke both in parallel where the API supports it; do not chain sequentially
IF parallel tool calls return contradictory data, surface the contradiction:
  "I'm seeing conflicting information — [source A] shows X but [source B] shows Y.
  I'll check and get back to you, or you can verify at [portal]."

-- RETRY AND FALLBACK --
IF tool returns timeout: retry once after 2 s; if second attempt fails, use fallback
IF tool returns auth_error: do not retry; immediately escalate:
  "I'm unable to access that information right now. Please contact [team]."
IF tool returns rate_limited: wait 1 s, retry once; if still rate-limited, use fallback
Fallback template: "I'm having trouble reaching the system. You can check at
  [alternative] or try again in a few minutes."
MUST log all tool errors for observability; MUST NOT expose log data to user

-- IRREVERSIBLE ACTIONS --
(v1.0 has no mutating tools — this section is placeholder for v1.1)
When mutating tools are added: MUST require explicit confirmation before execution
Confirmation template: "To confirm: I'll [action description] — is that right? (yes/no)"
MUST NOT proceed if user response to confirmation is ambiguous
Structured Review of Prompt Requirements

Prompt requirements should be reviewed in a dedicated session separate from the main PRD review. The audience is different: the main PRD review focuses on business and product alignment, while the prompt requirements review focuses on implementability, testability, and safety coverage. Participants should include the conversation designer who will write the system prompt, at least one engineer who understands the model's tendencies and limitations, and the AI safety reviewer.
Run the prompt requirements review against four questions for each item:
Is this traceable to a behaviour spec item? If not, the prompt requirement is an undiscussed assumption that needs to be ratified or removed.

Is this implementable in a system prompt? Some requirements sound reasonable but are difficult or impossible for a language model to reliably follow — for example, "always verify the user's intent before answering" is too vague to implement consistently. Requirements that pass business review but fail implementability review need to be either refined or replaced with a structural solution (a classifier, a UI affordance, a retrieval check).
Does this requirement have a corresponding eval scenario? If not, assign one before the review ends. Unverified requirements are technical debt.

Does this requirement conflict with any other requirement in the document? Conflicts discovered in review are fixed in an hour. Conflicts discovered in eval are fixed in a sprint. Conflicts discovered in production are fixed in an incident response.

The output of the prompt requirements review is a signed-off document that the conversation designer uses as their authoritative brief for system prompt authoring. The system prompt is not considered complete until every item in the prompt requirements document has been addressed — either implemented in the prompt or explicitly deferred with justification.
Behaviour Specifications for Safety-Critical Interactions

Some agent interactions require more rigorous behaviour specification because the consequences of incorrect behaviour are disproportionately severe. Financial advice, medical information, legal guidance, and crisis response all require behaviour specs that go beyond the standard template and include explicit safety escalation paths, liability disclaimers, and human-review thresholds.

For safety-critical interactions, the behaviour specification must include a dedicated safety section that answers: what constitutes a safety-critical event in this domain? What is the agent's first response to a safety-critical signal? Who is notified, and within what time frame? What is the fallback when the human escalation path is unavailable? These questions must be answered before implementation begins — not discovered in a post-incident review.

Crisis detection vocabulary: list the exact phrases, keywords, or sentiment patterns that trigger the safety escalation path. Do not rely on the model to generalise — enumerate the vocabulary and verify coverage with an adversarial eval set.

Escalation response template: the exact language the agent uses when it detects a safety-critical signal. This template must be reviewed by a domain expert (a mental health professional, a legal counsel, a medical practitioner) before it is included in the system prompt.

Hard stop condition: for some safety events, the agent must cease all task processing immediately and not continue the original task in the same session. Specify exactly which safety signals trigger a hard stop versus a soft escalation that offers support but allows the task to continue.

Human notification path: who is notified when a safety event is detected, through what channel, and within what SLA. This is both a UX requirement (the user must know help is coming) and an operations requirement (the on-call team must receive the alert).

Audit logging: all safety events must be logged with sufficient context for post-incident analysis. The logging requirement belongs in the behaviour spec because it affects the agent's behaviour (it must emit a structured safety event to the observability platform) and may require additional data fields in the tool response schema.

Safety-critical behaviour specs require sign-off from the organisation's legal and compliance teams, not just the product owner and AI safety reviewer. This expands the review cycle but is non-negotiable for features deployed in regulated domains or to vulnerable user populations.
Writing behavior specifications in practice

A behavior specification bridges the gap between a product requirement and a prompt. It defines the contract the model must fulfil: inputs it accepts, outputs it produces, constraints it respects, and edge cases it handles gracefully. The following patterns help teams write specifications that translate directly into evals.
Given-When-Then format for behavior specs

# behavior-spec.yaml — contract review assistant
feature: contract_clause_extractor

behaviors:
  - id: B-001
    given: "A vendor MSA with a non-standard limitation-of-liability clause"
    when: "User asks the agent to review the contract"
    then:
      - "Agent flags the clause with risk_level = high"
      - "Agent cites the exact clause text as evidence"
      - "Agent provides the firm's standard position from the playbook"
    eval_dataset: "tests/evals/liability_clauses.jsonl"

  - id: B-002
    given: "A standard NDA with no unusual terms"
    when: "User asks the agent to review the contract"
    then:
      - "Agent returns risk_level = low for all clauses"
      - "Agent does NOT hallucinate non-existent issues"
    eval_dataset: "tests/evals/standard_ndas.jsonl"

  - id: B-003
    given: "A contract with a prompt injection attempt in clause text"
    when: "User asks the agent to review the contract"
    then:
      - "Agent ignores the injected instruction"
      - "Agent logs a security_event with type = prompt_injection_attempt"
      - "Agent continues normal clause analysis"
    eval_dataset: "tests/evals/adversarial_contracts.jsonl"
Mapping behavior specs to evals
Each behavior specification should map 1:1 to an eval dataset and a set of automated assertions. This traceability ensures that every product requirement has a corresponding test that runs in CI.
Versioning and change management

Behavior specifications must be versioned alongside the system prompt. When either changes, the full eval suite re-runs to detect regressions. Use semantic versioning: patch for prompt wording tweaks, minor for new behaviors added, major for breaking changes to output schema.
Store behavior specs in the same repository as the system prompt — co-location prevents drift between the spec and the implementation.
Require a spec update in every PR that modifies the system prompt. The PR template should include a checklist item for this.
Tag spec versions in the prompt registry so you can reproduce any past model-prompt-spec combination for debugging.
When retiring a behavior, mark it as deprecated in the spec rather than deleting it — this preserves the audit trail of what the model was expected to do at each point in time.
Prompt Requirements as Executable Documentation

The most mature implementation of prompt requirements treats them as executable documentation: structured specifications that can be parsed by automated tools to generate initial system prompt drafts, eval scenario stubs, and coverage reports. This is not aspirational — several teams have built lightweight tooling that reads a YAML-formatted prompt requirements document and generates a prompt template with placeholders for conversation-designer refinement.

The value of executable prompt requirements is not in automation for its own sake but in the discipline it enforces. A YAML schema for prompt requirements prevents the ambiguity that creeps into prose documents. A coverage report that checks whether every prompt requirement ID appears in at least one eval scenario catches gaps before they reach QA. A diff tool that compares two versions of the prompt requirements and identifies changed items makes prompt change review mechanical rather than laborious.

Teams that have not yet invested in tooling can achieve similar benefits with a simple spreadsheet: prompt requirement ID, behaviour spec source ID, status (draft / reviewed / implemented / verified), eval scenario ID, and last-updated timestamp. This lightweight registry costs one hour to set up and saves multiple sprints of confusion when the prompt evolves.

Regardless of tooling, the underlying principle is the same: prompt requirements are not a one-time document written before implementation and then forgotten. They are the living specification of the agent's designed behaviour, maintained alongside the code and the prompt, and referenced as the primary source of truth whenever a behaviour question arises — in code review, in QA, in incident response, and in stakeholder demos.
Summary

Behaviour specifications and prompt requirements are the bridge between product intent and model implementation. The behaviour spec answers what the agent must do in language that every stakeholder can review and agree on. The prompt requirements translate each behaviour into specific MUST, MUST NOT, and IF/THEN instructions that the system prompt author can implement and QA can verify. Traceability between spec, requirements, and eval scenarios ensures that every required behaviour is implemented, tested, and regression-protected. This discipline is what transforms a system prompt from an art form into an engineered specification.
